passages.explanation: The chapter opens with Lord Henry announcing Dorian's engagement to Basil, and their contrasting reactions reveal the power dynamics at play. Basil's shock and dismay stem from his possessive devotion to Dorian, while Henry's casual amusement shows his view of people as entertainment. Wilde sets up the tension between art, love, and ownership that will define the chapter.

The rapid-fire dialogue about marriage exposes each character's philosophy. Lord Henry treats marriage as an institution to be mocked, reducing it to wit and paradox, while Basil worries about class and propriety. Wilde is satirizing Victorian social attitudes while also showing how Henry uses cleverness as a shield against genuine feeling, turning every human institution into an epigram.

Lord Henry's long speech about marriage, individualism, and experience is the philosophical heart of the chapter. He frames Dorian's engagement as a potential experiment rather than a commitment, revealing his view of people as aesthetic objects to study. Basil pushes back, but Henry dismisses optimism as terror in disguise. Wilde explores the seductive danger of treating life as a series of sensations rather than moral choices.

Dorian bursts in radiating pure romantic joy, and the contrast with the cynicism that preceded him is almost painful. His breathless account of falling in love with Sibyl is drenched in theatrical metaphor, revealing that he loves her not as a person but as an embodiment of Shakespeare's heroines. Wilde signals that Dorian's love is already more about art and beauty than about another human being, foreshadowing disaster.

Lord Henry probes the details of the proposal with surgical precision, and Dorian's idealism begins to show its cracks. His declaration that Sibyl is 'everything' to him is undercut by the fact that he describes her entirely in terms of the roles she plays. The exchange about pleasure being Nature's test introduces the hedonistic philosophy that will increasingly govern Dorian's choices.

The dinner-table debate about goodness, individualism, and morality is Wilde at his most provocative. Henry argues that being good means being in harmony with oneself rather than with society, an argument that sounds liberating but conveniently strips away all responsibility to others. Basil warns that self-indulgence carries a price in remorse and degradation, essentially prophesying the entire novel's trajectory.

The argument about love, adoration, and what women 'give' men reveals the objectification lurking beneath Dorian's romantic idealism. When he insists women give men 'the very gold of their lives,' Henry counters that they demand it back in small change. Wilde exposes how both the romantic and the cynic reduce women to functions, one idealizing them as muses and the other dismissing them as nuisances.

The chapter's closing movement is rich with foreboding. Henry's declaration that acting is 'more real than life' perfectly encapsulates the aesthetic philosophy that will destroy Sibyl. Basil's silent grief as he rides alone in the cab, feeling that 'life had come between them,' conveys the tragedy of someone who loves genuinely watching that love become irrelevant. The final image of Basil feeling he has aged years captures how Dorian's transformation is already causing collateral damage.
